comments.text: The behavior of $F$ is the subject of [30]. It's trivial that $F(N + k) \leq F(N) + F(k)$ for all $N$ and $k$. Let $g(N)$ be maximal such that there exist Sidon sets $A, B \subseteq \{1,...,N\}$ such that $(A - A) \cap (B - B) = \{0\}$ and $g(N) \leq |A|, |B|$. Clearly, $g(N) \leq F(N)$ for all $N$; I don't know if Erdős ever asked about the behavior of $g$, but two related problems are [42] and [43]. (EDIT: Also [331].) One nice partial result on this problem is that if $N + 1 \geq k$, then $F(N + k) \leq F(N) + g(k)$. To prove this, let $A \subseteq \{1,..., N + k\}$ be a maximum-size Sidon set. By definition of $g$, the set $A$ has at most $g(k)$-many elements in $\{1,...,k\}$ or it has at most $g(k)$-many elements in $\{N+1,..., N+k\}$ (or both). By removing this interval, we then get a Sidon set of size at least $|A| - g(k) = F(N + k) - g(k)$ on an interval of length $N$, which completes the proof.
